text:
`flatten.js` now flattens to any depth. The previous version only unwrapped one level; the new one recurses, so a value nested arbitrarily deep ends up in the flat output.

How it works: it walks each element of the input array. If the element is itself an array, it recurses with `flatten(x)` and pushes each result; otherwise it pushes the value as-is. Because the array branch calls `flatten` again rather than just copying one level, nesting of any depth — `[1, [2, [3, [4]]]]` → `[1, 2, 3, 4]` — collapses fully. That's the change that makes the deep-case tests pass where a single-level implementation would leave inner arrays intact.

Behavior worth knowing, from reading the code:
- Empty nested arrays disappear entirely (they contribute no elements), which is the conventional flatten behavior.
- Non-array values of any type pass straight through, including `undefined` and objects.
- Depth is bounded only by the JavaScript call stack, so a pathologically deep input (tens of thousands of levels) could overflow — not a concern for normal test cases.

I based this on reading the source only; I did not run the file or the test suite, so I can't report a passing run firsthand — the reasoning above is a code review of the logic, not an executed result.
